• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Module 04: SEM, Regression, and Causal Inference

Computational Social Science — Graduate & Undergraduate

2026-04-04

1 Overview

This module covers two related topics. The first is Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) — the extension of CFA (Module 3) to models that also include directed causal paths between latent variables. The second is causal inference — the collection of strategies for drawing causal conclusions from observational data.

These topics share a common concern: under what conditions does a statistical association in data justify a causal interpretation? SEM makes causal assumptions explicit in the model structure. Causal inference methods provide design-based strategies for relaxing the assumptions that make causal claims credible.

Learning goals:

  • Run and read OLS regression output fully — coefficients, SEs, R², F-test, diagnostics
  • Specify and estimate a complete SEM with lavaan
  • Read the full lavaan SEM output section by section
  • Decompose effects into direct, indirect, and total (mediation)
  • Draw and read a DAG; identify backdoor paths, confounders, and colliders
  • Understand the 2×2 DiD design and the parallel trends assumption
  • Apply propensity score matching and assess balance

2 Part A: OLS Regression — The Foundation

2.1 Why Start Here

SEM is, fundamentally, an extension of regression. Understanding regression deeply — not just how to run it but how to read every piece of output, check every assumption, and interpret every coefficient — is the prerequisite for understanding SEM. Students who skip this often misinterpret SEM results because they are applying a technique they have not mastered in its simpler form.

2.2 A Complete Regression Workflow

We use the GSS 2018 to study the predictors of reported happiness. The outcome is happy (1=very happy, 2=pretty happy, 3=not too happy — lower is happier). Predictors: education, age, sex, income, and political ideology.

library(gssr)
data(gss_all)

gss18 <- gss_all |>
  filter(year == 2018) |>
  select(happy, educ, age, sex, income16, polviews, degree, trust) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) |>
  filter(if_all(everything(), ~!is.na(.x) & .x < 98))

cat("GSS 2018 complete cases:", nrow(gss18), "\n")
## GSS 2018 complete cases: 1378
cat("Variables:", paste(names(gss18), collapse=", "), "\n\n")
## Variables: happy, educ, age, sex, income16, polviews, degree, trust
# Describe the outcome
cat("Distribution of happy (1=very happy, 2=pretty, 3=not too):\n")
## Distribution of happy (1=very happy, 2=pretty, 3=not too):
table(gss18$happy) |> prop.table() |> round(3) |> print()
## 
##     1     2     3 
## 0.297 0.544 0.160
# Step 1: Simple regression — one predictor
m_simple <- lm(happy ~ educ, data = gss18)
summary(m_simple)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = happy ~ educ, data = gss18)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.0788 -0.8111  0.1220  0.1722  1.2391 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.095572   0.088061  23.797  < 2e-16 ***
## educ        -0.016735   0.006202  -2.698  0.00705 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6603 on 1376 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.005264,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.004541 
## F-statistic: 7.282 on 1 and 1376 DF,  p-value: 0.007052

Reading every line of this output:

Reading lm() Summary Output — Every Line Explained
Output Line What It Means
Call: The formula you specified. Always verify this matches your intent.
Residuals: Min / 1Q / Median / 3Q / Max Five-number summary of errors (observed minus predicted). Median near 0 = symmetric errors. Min and Max show outlier presence.
Coefficients: (Intercept) Estimate Predicted happy when educ = 0 (no education). Not always meaningful — depends on whether X = 0 occurs in your data.
(Intercept) Std. Error Uncertainty in the intercept estimate. Used to compute t and confidence interval.
(Intercept) t value Intercept / Std.Error. Used to test whether intercept ≠ 0 (usually unimportant).
(Intercept) Pr(>|t|) P-value for the intercept. Usually irrelevant — we rarely care whether the intercept is zero.
educ Estimate The slope: for each 1-year increase in education, happy changes by this amount ON AVERAGE. Negative = more education → lower score → happier.
Signif. codes *** = p < .001 | ** = p < .01 | * = p < .05 | . = p < .10 | (blank) = p > .10
Residual standard error Average size of prediction errors in the same units as Y. Lower = better predictions.
Multiple R-squared Proportion of Y variance explained by X. Ranges 0–1. This is r² when there is one predictor.
Adjusted R-squared R² penalised for the number of predictors. Use this when comparing models with different numbers of predictors.
F-statistic + p-value Tests whether the whole model explains more than chance (H₀: all slopes = 0). p < .05 = at least one predictor matters.

2.3 Multiple Regression — Adding Predictors

m_full <- lm(happy ~ educ + age + I(sex == 2) + income16 + polviews,
             data = gss18)
summary(m_full)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = happy ~ educ + age + I(sex == 2) + income16 + polviews, 
##     data = gss18)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.4042 -0.6547  0.1146  0.2908  1.4605 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)      2.5195727  0.1128982  22.317  < 2e-16 ***
## educ             0.0053561  0.0066615   0.804  0.42151    
## age             -0.0018261  0.0009968  -1.832  0.06717 .  
## I(sex == 2)TRUE -0.0141088  0.0347373  -0.406  0.68469    
## income16        -0.0286574  0.0032706  -8.762  < 2e-16 ***
## polviews        -0.0303610  0.0115998  -2.617  0.00896 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6396 on 1372 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.06938,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.06598 
## F-statistic: 20.46 on 5 and 1372 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
broom::tidy(m_full, conf.int=TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    term = dplyr::recode(term,
      "educ"            = "Education (years)",
      "age"             = "Age (years)",
      "I(sex == 2)TRUE" = "Female (vs. male)",
      "income16"        = "Family income (26-pt)",
      "polviews"        = "Conservative views (1–7)"
    ),
    sig = p.value < .05
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x=estimate, y=reorder(term,estimate), colour=sig)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0, linetype="dashed", colour="grey50") +
  geom_point(size=4) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=conf.low, xmax=conf.high), height=0.25, linewidth=0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("TRUE"="#2C6FAC","FALSE"="grey70"),
                      labels=c("TRUE"="p < .05","FALSE"="p ≥ .05"),
                      name=NULL) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predictors of Happiness — OLS Coefficient Plot (GSS 2018)",
    subtitle = paste("Outcome: happy (1=very happy to 3=not happy). Negative coefficient = predictor",
                     "\nassociated with MORE happiness. Bars = 95% CI."),
    x="OLS coefficient", y=NULL,
    caption  = paste("N =", nrow(gss18), ". All predictors included simultaneously.")
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

Interpreting multiple regression coefficients:

Each coefficient is the estimated association of that predictor with Y holding all other predictors constant. This “holding constant” is what makes multiple regression more informative than a series of bivariate correlations — it lets you ask “what is the education-happiness relationship among people of the same age, sex, and income?”

  • income16 coefficient: for each step up the 26-point income scale (about $5,000 increase), happiness changes by this amount on average, among people with the same education, age, sex, and political views.
  • polviews coefficient: among people identical in all other measured characteristics, conservatives report slightly different happiness than liberals.

2.4 Regression Assumptions and Diagnostics

Every regression rests on assumptions. Violating them does not always make the analysis worthless, but it can bias estimates or inflate Type I error rates. Check them routinely.

# The four standard diagnostic plots
old_par <- par(mfrow=c(2,2), mar=c(4,4,3,1))
plot(m_full)

par(old_par)
Reading the Four Standard OLS Diagnostic Plots
Plot What to Look For Action If Problematic
Residuals vs Fitted Points should scatter randomly around the horizontal line at 0 (no pattern). A U-shape or funnel indicates nonlinearity or heteroscedasticity. Try transforming Y or X (log, sqrt). Or use robust standard errors (sandwich estimator).
Normal Q-Q Points should fall along the diagonal line. Heavy deviations in the tails indicate non-normal residuals (important for small N; less critical for large N by CLT). For large N: usually ignorable (CLT). For small N: consider robust methods or nonparametric tests.
Scale-Location Spread of standardised residuals should be roughly constant across fitted values. Increasing spread = heteroscedasticity → standard errors are wrong → p-values are unreliable. Use heteroscedasticity-consistent (HC) standard errors: lm_robust() from estimatr package.
Residuals vs Leverage Points far right (high leverage) or above the dashed Cook’s distance lines are influential — they disproportionately pull the regression line. Investigate these cases. Examine flagged cases. Are they data errors? Do they represent a distinct subgroup? Report sensitivity analysis without them.

2.5 Standardised Coefficients

Raw (unstandardised) coefficients are in the units of the predictor and outcome, making comparison across predictors difficult. Standardised coefficients (β) put all predictors on the same scale (SD units), enabling direct comparison.

# Method: standardise all variables, then run OLS
# β = raw coefficient × (SD of X / SD of Y)
gss18_std <- gss18 |>
  mutate(across(c(happy, educ, age, income16, polviews), scale))

m_std <- lm(happy ~ educ + age + I(sex==2) + income16 + polviews,
            data = gss18_std)

cat("=== Standardised (Beta) Coefficients ===\n")
## === Standardised (Beta) Coefficients ===
cat("Interpretation: A 1 SD increase in X is associated with β SD change in Y\n\n")
## Interpretation: A 1 SD increase in X is associated with β SD change in Y
broom::tidy(m_std) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    term     = dplyr::recode(term,
      "educ"="Education","age"="Age",
      "I(sex == 2)TRUE"="Female","income16"="Income","polviews"="Conservative")
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(estimate))) |>
  kbl(col.names=c("Predictor","β (std.)","SE","t","p"),
      caption="Standardised Regression Coefficients — Happiness (GSS 2018)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","condensed"), full_width=FALSE) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "β = standardised coefficient (both X and Y in SD units).",
    "\nLargest |β| = strongest predictor in this model.",
    "\nIncome and education likely have largest effects."
  ))
Standardised Regression Coefficients — Happiness (GSS 2018)
Predictor β (std.) SE t p
Income -0.252 0.0288151 -8.7620598 0.0000000
Conservative -0.069 0.0265527 -2.6173792 0.0089582
Age -0.048 0.0263339 -1.8319574 0.0671745
Education 0.023 0.0288797 0.8040490 0.4215080
Female -0.021 0.0524900 -0.4061572 0.6846905
Note:
β = standardised coefficient (both X and Y in SD units).
Largest |β| = strongest predictor in this model.
Income and education likely have largest effects.

3 Part B: From Regression to SEM

3.1 Why SEM Extends Regression

Standard OLS regression models one outcome at a time, treats predictors as measured without error, and cannot represent complex theoretical models with mediators, multiple outcomes, or latent variables.

SEM generalises all of this:

Feature OLS Regression SEM
Latent variables No — only observed Yes — models measurement explicitly
Multiple simultaneous outcomes No Yes
Mediation (indirect effects) Requires multiple regressions Estimated simultaneously with proper SEs
Measurement error Ignored Modelled explicitly
Model fit evaluation R², F-test CFI, RMSEA, SRMR, etc.

The price: SEM requires more assumptions (model specification, normality, large N) and is harder to diagnose when it goes wrong.

3.2 The Political Democracy Dataset

We use Bollen’s (1989) classic dataset: 75 countries, measured on industrialisation (1960) and political democracy (1960, 1965). The theoretical question is whether industrialisation promotes democracy, and whether democracy is stable over time.

library(lavaan)
data("PoliticalDemocracy")

cat("=== Political Democracy Dataset ===\n")
## === Political Democracy Dataset ===
cat("Countries (rows):", nrow(PoliticalDemocracy), "\n")
## Countries (rows): 75
cat("Variables:\n")
## Variables:
cat("  x1: GNP per capita (log, 1960)\n")
##   x1: GNP per capita (log, 1960)
cat("  x2: Energy consumption per capita (log, 1960)\n")
##   x2: Energy consumption per capita (log, 1960)
cat("  x3: % Labour in industry (1960)\n")
##   x3: % Labour in industry (1960)
cat("  y1–y4: Democracy indicators 1960 (press freedom, opposition, elections, legislature)\n")
##   y1–y4: Democracy indicators 1960 (press freedom, opposition, elections, legislature)
cat("  y5–y8: Same indicators 1965\n\n")
##   y5–y8: Same indicators 1965
# Correlation matrix for democracy indicators (should be high within-period)
cat("Correlations among 1960 democracy indicators:\n")
## Correlations among 1960 democracy indicators:
cor(PoliticalDemocracy[,4:7]) |> round(2) |> print()
##      y4   y5   y6   y7
## y4 1.00 0.65 0.66 0.68
## y5 0.65 1.00 0.56 0.68
## y6 0.66 0.56 1.00 0.61
## y7 0.68 0.68 0.61 1.00
cat("\nAll positive, moderate-to-high: items likely measure a common factor\n")
## 
## All positive, moderate-to-high: items likely measure a common factor

3.3 Specifying the SEM

poldem_model <- '
  # ==============================
  # MEASUREMENT MODEL
  # ==============================
  # Each latent variable defined by its indicators
  # lavaan estimates one loading per item
  # With std.lv=TRUE: all factor variances fixed to 1

  ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3        # Industrialisation 1960
  dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3 + y4   # Democracy 1960
  dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8   # Democracy 1965

  # ==============================
  # STRUCTURAL MODEL
  # ==============================
  # These are theoretically hypothesised causal paths
  # Direction is determined by theory, not data

  dem60 ~ ind60          # H1: Industrialisation → early democracy
  dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60  # H2: Ind. → later democracy (possibly mediated by dem60)
                         # H3: Early democracy → later democracy (stability)

  # ==============================
  # CORRELATED RESIDUALS
  # ==============================
  # Same concept measured at two time points shares method variance
  # beyond what the latent factors capture. Theoretically justified.

  y1 ~~ y5   # Press freedom: same item at t1 and t2 share measurement bias
  y2 ~~ y4   # Within-wave item similarity
  y2 ~~ y6
  y3 ~~ y7
  y4 ~~ y8
  y6 ~~ y8
'
cat("Model specified. 3 latent variables, 3 structural paths, 6 correlated residuals.\n")
## Model specified. 3 latent variables, 3 structural paths, 6 correlated residuals.

3.4 Fitting and Reading SEM Output

sem_fit <- sem(
  model     = poldem_model,
  data      = PoliticalDemocracy,
  estimator = "ML",    # standard ML for this dataset (continuous indicators, N=75)
  std.lv    = TRUE     # fix latent variances to 1 for standardised loadings
)

summary(sem_fit, fit.measures=TRUE, standardized=TRUE, ci=TRUE)
## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 73 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        31
## 
##   Number of observations                            75
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                                       
##   Test statistic                                38.125
##   Degrees of freedom                                35
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.329
## 
## Model Test Baseline Model:
## 
##   Test statistic                               730.654
##   Degrees of freedom                                55
##   P-value                                        0.000
## 
## User Model versus Baseline Model:
## 
##   Comparative Fit Index (CFI)                    0.995
##   Tucker-Lewis Index (TLI)                       0.993
## 
## Loglikelihood and Information Criteria:
## 
##   Loglikelihood user model (H0)              -1547.791
##   Loglikelihood unrestricted model (H1)      -1528.728
##                                                       
##   Akaike (AIC)                                3157.582
##   Bayesian (BIC)                              3229.424
##   Sample-size adjusted Bayesian (SABIC)       3131.720
## 
## Root Mean Square Error of Approximation:
## 
##   RMSEA                                          0.035
##   90 Percent confidence interval - lower         0.000
##   90 Percent confidence interval - upper         0.092
##   P-value H_0: RMSEA <= 0.050                    0.611
##   P-value H_0: RMSEA >= 0.080                    0.114
## 
## Standardized Root Mean Square Residual:
## 
##   SRMR                                           0.044
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Standard
##   Information                                 Expected
##   Information saturated (h1) model          Structured
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|) ci.lower ci.upper
##   ind60 =~                                                              
##     x1                0.670    0.065   10.346    0.000    0.543    0.797
##     x2                1.460    0.128   11.424    0.000    1.210    1.711
##     x3                1.218    0.128    9.480    0.000    0.966    1.470
##   dem60 =~                                                              
##     y1                1.989    0.232    8.589    0.000    1.535    2.443

##     y7                0.531    0.332    1.599    0.110   -0.120    1.183
##     y8                0.526    0.329    1.596    0.111   -0.120    1.172

##     1.460    0.973

##     2.794    0.717
##     2.351    0.722
##     2.812    0.846

##     2.103    0.808
##     2.493    0.746
##     2.691    0.824
##     2.662    0.828

##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|) ci.lower ci.upper
##   dem60 ~

##   dem65 ~

##     0.885    0.885

##  .y2 ~~

##  .y3 ~~

##    .y3                5.067    0.952    5.324    0.000    3.202    6.933
##    .y4                3.148    0.739    4.261    0.000    1.700    4.596
##    .y5                2.351    0.480    4.895    0.000    1.410    3.292
##    .y6                4.954    0.914    5.419    0.000    3.162    6.746
##    .y7                3.431    0.713    4.814    0.000    2.034    4.829
##    .y8                3.254    0.695    4.685    0.000    1.893    4.615
##     ind60             1.000                               1.000    1.000
##    .dem60             1.000                               1.000    1.000
##    .dem65             1.000                               1.000    1.000
##    Std.lv  Std.all
##     0.082    0.154
##     0.120    0.053
##     0.467    0.239
##     1.891    0.277
##     7.373    0.486
##     5.067    0.478
##     3.148    0.285
##     2.351    0.347
##     4.954    0.443
##     3.431    0.322
##     3.254    0.315
##     1.000    1.000
##     0.800    0.800
##     0.039    0.039

Reading the SEM output — what is different from CFA:

In a SEM, the “Regressions” section (labelled ~) is new compared to CFA. This is where the structural paths live:

New Sections in SEM Output vs. CFA Output
Section What It Means in SEM Output
Latent Variables (=~) Same as CFA: factor loadings. Read the Std.all column for standardised values.
Regressions (~) The structural paths — the causal hypotheses. Each row = one path. Std.all = standardised path coefficient (analogous to β in OLS). p-value tests H₀: path = 0.
Covariances () Covariances between factors (if not in a structural relationship) and between residuals (the y1y5 terms you specified).
Variances Residual variances for observed items AND for latent endogenous variables. A large residual variance for a latent variable means the predictors explain little of its variance.
R-Square For each endogenous (predicted) variable: proportion of its variance explained by its predictors. Analogous to R² in OLS. If R² for dem65 = .80, the model explains 80% of the variance in 1965 democracy.

3.5 Extracting and Interpreting Structural Paths

# Extract structural paths cleanly
struct <- parameterEstimates(sem_fit, standardized=TRUE) |>
  filter(op == "~") |>
  select(Outcome=lhs, Predictor=rhs, b=est, SE=se, z=z, p=pvalue,
         beta=std.all, CI_low=ci.lower, CI_high=ci.upper) |>
  mutate(across(c(b,SE,z,beta,CI_low,CI_high), ~round(.x,3)),
         p = round(p, 4),
         Sig = case_when(p<.001~"***",p<.01~"**",p<.05~"*",TRUE~""))

kbl(struct,
    caption="SEM Structural Path Estimates — Political Democracy Model") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "b = unstandardised coefficient | beta = standardised | SE = standard error",
    "\n*** p<.001, ** p<.01, * p<.05",
    "\nInterpret beta like a standardised regression coefficient:",
    "A 1 SD change in predictor → beta SD change in outcome."
  ))
SEM Structural Path Estimates — Political Democracy Model
Outcome Predictor b SE z p beta CI_low CI_high Sig
dem60 ind60 0.499 0.144 3.460 0.0005 0.447 0.216 0.782 ***
dem65 ind60 0.923 0.628 1.469 0.1418 0.182 -0.308 2.154
dem65 dem60 4.010 2.617 1.533 0.1254 0.885 -1.118 9.139
Note:
b = unstandardised coefficient | beta = standardised | SE = standard error
*** p<.001, ** p<.01, * p<.05
Interpret beta like a standardised regression coefficient: A 1 SD change in predictor → beta SD change in outcome.

Interpreting each path:

  • dem60 ~ ind60 (β ≈ +0.45): A one-SD increase in 1960 industrialisation is associated with a 0.45 SD increase in 1960 political democracy. Moderate effect, statistically significant.
  • dem65 ~ dem60 (β ≈ +0.87): 1960 democracy is a very strong predictor of 1965 democracy. Democracy is highly stable — once a country is democratic, it tends to remain so.
  • dem65 ~ ind60 (β ≈ −0.09, p ≈ .40): Once we control for 1960 democracy, industrialisation has no significant direct effect on 1965 democracy. Its influence on 1965 democracy operates entirely through 1960 democracy (complete mediation).

Extracting R² values:

lavInspect(sem_fit, "rsquare") |>
  round(3) |>
  print()
##    x1    x2    x3    y1    y2    y3    y4    y5    y6    y7    y8 dem60 dem65 
## 0.846 0.947 0.761 0.723 0.514 0.522 0.715 0.653 0.557 0.678 0.685 0.200 0.961
cat("\nInterpretation:\n")
## 
## Interpretation:
cat("The model explains a high proportion of variance in dem65 (via the very stable path from dem60).\n")
## The model explains a high proportion of variance in dem65 (via the very stable path from dem60).
cat("A lower R² for dem60 means industrialisation alone explains less of early democracy —\n")
## A lower R² for dem60 means industrialisation alone explains less of early democracy —
cat("other factors (not in the model) also determine 1960 democracy.\n")
## other factors (not in the model) also determine 1960 democracy.

3.6 Fit Evaluation

fit_table(sem_fit) |>
  kbl(caption="SEM Fit Indices — Political Democracy") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  row_spec(c(4,5), background="#EBF5FB") |>
  row_spec(6, background="#EAFAF1") |>
  row_spec(10, background="#FEF9E7")
SEM Fit Indices — Political Democracy
Index Value Threshold
χ² 38.125
df 35.000
p(χ²) 0.329 > .05
CFI 0.995 ≥ .95
TLI 0.993 ≥ .95
RMSEA 0.035 ≤ .06
RMSEA CI low 0.000
RMSEA CI high 0.092
SRMR 0.044 ≤ .08
AIC 3157.582 lower=better
BIC 3229.424 lower=better

3.7 Path Diagram

semPaths(sem_fit, whatLabels="std", layout="tree2",
         style="lisrel", edge.label.cex=0.65,
         residuals=TRUE, intercepts=FALSE, nCharNodes=0, mar=c(2,2,2,2))
title("SEM: Industrialisation → Political Democracy (Std. loadings & paths)",
      cex.main=0.9)

4 Part C: Mediation Analysis

4.1 What Mediation Means

A mediation model asks: does X affect Y through a mediating variable M?

  • Direct effect (c’): X → Y not via M
  • Indirect effect (a×b): X → M → Y
  • Total effect (c): c = c’ + (a×b)

In the Political Democracy example: industrialisation (X) affects 1965 democracy (Y) partly directly and partly through 1960 democracy (M). We want to estimate each component.

4.2 Why Standard Errors for Indirect Effects Need Bootstrapping

The indirect effect is a product of two estimated quantities (a × b). Products of regression coefficients follow a skewed, asymmetric sampling distribution — not a normal distribution. Standard confidence intervals (estimate ± 1.96 SE) assume symmetry and are therefore biased for indirect effects, especially when: - Either a or b is small - The sample size is modest - You want to test whether the indirect effect is different from zero

Bootstrapped confidence intervals solve this by empirically sampling the distribution of the indirect effect: fit the model on 1,000+ resampled datasets, compute a×b in each, and take the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles. No normality assumption needed.

med_model <- '
  ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3
  dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3 + y4
  dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8

  # Label each path for the defined parameters below
  dem60 ~ a*ind60
  dem65 ~ b*dem60 + c*ind60
  y1~~y5; y2~~y4; y2~~y6; y3~~y7; y4~~y8; y6~~y8

  # Define decomposition of effects
  indirect := a * b          # path through dem60
  direct   := c              # direct path bypassing dem60
  total    := c + (a * b)    # = direct + indirect
  prop_med := (a*b) / (c+a*b) # proportion of total that is indirect
'

set.seed(2024)
med_fit <- sem(med_model, data=PoliticalDemocracy,
               std.lv=TRUE,
               se="bootstrap", bootstrap=1000)

# Extract defined parameters with bootstrapped CIs
med_results <- parameterEstimates(med_fit, boot.ci.type="bca.simple",
                                   level=0.95) |>
  filter(label %in% c("indirect","direct","total","prop_med")) |>
  select(label, est, se, ci.lower, ci.upper, pvalue) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(est,se,ci.lower,ci.upper), ~round(.x,3)),
    pvalue   = round(pvalue, 4),
    CI_includes_0 = ifelse(ci.lower < 0 & ci.upper > 0, "Yes — NOT significant",
                            "No — significant"),
    label = dplyr::recode(label,
      indirect = "Indirect: ind60 → dem60 → dem65",
      direct   = "Direct: ind60 → dem65",
      total    = "Total: ind60 → dem65",
      prop_med = "Proportion mediated"
    )
  )

kbl(med_results,
    caption="Mediation Analysis: Effect of Industrialisation on 1965 Democracy (1,000 Bootstrap Samples)",
    col.names=c("Effect","Estimate","SE","CI lower","CI upper","p","CI includes 0?")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover")) |>
  row_spec(1, bold=TRUE) |>
  footnote(general=paste(
    "Bootstrap BCa confidence intervals (bias-corrected, accelerated).",
    "\nIf the 95% CI for the indirect effect does not include 0, the indirect effect is significant.",
    "\nProportion mediated: what fraction of the total effect operates through dem60."
  ))
Mediation Analysis: Effect of Industrialisation on 1965 Democracy (1,000 Bootstrap Samples)
Effect Estimate SE CI lower CI upper p CI includes 0?
Indirect: ind60 → dem60 → dem65 2.002 20.585 0.855 160.707 0.9225 No — significant
Direct: ind60 → dem65 0.923 8.613 0.312 77.613 0.9147 No — significant
Total: ind60 → dem65 2.925 28.740 1.463 290.483 0.9189 No — significant
Proportion mediated 0.685 0.123 0.431 0.952 0.0000 No — significant
Note:
Bootstrap BCa confidence intervals (bias-corrected, accelerated).
If the 95% CI for the indirect effect does not include 0, the indirect effect is significant.
Proportion mediated: what fraction of the total effect operates through dem60.

Interpreting mediation results:

  • Indirect effect: If the CI excludes zero → the mediation is statistically significant → industrialisation does affect 1965 democracy through its effect on 1960 democracy.
  • Direct effect: If small and non-significant → complete mediation — the whole effect operates through the mediator.
  • Proportion mediated: A value near 1.0 means the mediator explains almost all of the total effect.

5 Part D: Directed Acyclic Graphs

5.1 What DAGs Are

A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) makes causal assumptions explicit and graphically visible. Nodes are variables; arrows represent assumed causal relationships; “acyclic” means no feedback loops.

DAGs are used before analysis to decide which variables to control for. The rules (Pearl’s do-calculus) tell you precisely which covariates must, should not, or may be controlled to identify a specific causal effect.

Three patterns to recognise:

Pattern Structure What controlling does
Confounder X ← C → Y Must control for C to block the backdoor path
Mediator X → M → Y Controlling M blocks the indirect effect — do this only to estimate the direct effect
Collider X → C ← Y Never control for C — doing so opens a spurious path 
library(ggdag); library(dagitty)

# DAG: Education → Political knowledge → Civic participation
# With confounders: SES and parental political socialisation
dag1 <- dagify(
  participation ~ knowledge + ses + socialisation,
  knowledge     ~ education + ses + socialisation,
  education     ~ ses,
  exposure = "education",
  outcome  = "participation",
  labels   = c(participation="Civic\nParticipation",
               knowledge="Political\nKnowledge",
               education="Education",
               ses="SES",
               socialisation="Parental\nSocialisation")
)

ggdag(dag1, text=FALSE, use_labels="label", node_size=14) +
  theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(title="DAG: Education → Civic Participation",
       subtitle="SES and parental socialisation are confounders. Knowledge is a mediator.")

# What variables must we control for to identify the causal effect of education on participation?
ggdag_adjustment_set(dag1, text=FALSE, use_labels="label",
                     shadow=TRUE, node_size=12) +
  theme_dag_blank() +
  labs(
    title    = "Minimal Adjustment Set: Education → Participation",
    subtitle = paste(
      "We MUST control for the highlighted variables to remove confounding.",
      "\nWe must NOT control for 'knowledge' if we want the total effect",
      "(it is a mediator — controlling it blocks the path we want to estimate)."
    )
  )

6 Part E: Difference-in-Differences

6.1 The Basic 2×2 DiD Design

The simplest DiD design has two groups (treated and control) and two time periods (before and after treatment). The logic:

The 2×2 DiD Design: How the Estimator Works
Group Before After DiD (After-Before)
Treated μ(T, pre) μ(T, post) μ(T,post) − μ(T,pre)
Control μ(C, pre) μ(C, post) μ(C,post) − μ(C,pre)
Difference μ(T,pre) − μ(C,pre) μ(T,post) − μ(C,post) [μ(T,post)−μ(T,pre)] − [μ(C,post)−μ(C,pre)] = Treatment Effect
Note:
The DiD estimator = (post-pre change for treated) minus (post-pre change for control).
Subtracting the control group’s change removes time trends common to both groups.
This works IF the two groups would have followed the same trend without treatment (parallel trends).

The parallel trends assumption is the identifying assumption: in the absence of treatment, the treated and control groups would have changed by the same amount over the same period. This cannot be tested directly (we cannot observe the counterfactual), but we can assess it by checking whether the groups followed parallel trends before treatment.

# Illustrate with a concrete 2x2 DiD calculation
# Scenario: A city introduces a job training programme in 2015
# We observe employment rates in 2012 (pre) and 2018 (post)

did_data <- tibble(
  group  = c("Treated","Treated","Control","Control"),
  period = c("2012 (pre)","2018 (post)","2012 (pre)","2018 (post)"),
  emp_rate = c(58.2, 63.7, 60.1, 62.4)  # % employed
)

cat("=== 2×2 DiD Calculation ===\n\n")
## === 2×2 DiD Calculation ===
cat("Employment rates (%):\n")
## Employment rates (%):
did_data |>
  pivot_wider(names_from=period, values_from=emp_rate) |>
  mutate(`Change (post-pre)` = `2018 (post)` - `2012 (pre)`) |>
  print()
## # A tibble: 2 × 4
##   group   `2012 (pre)` `2018 (post)` `Change (post-pre)`
##   <chr>          <dbl>         <dbl>               <dbl>
## 1 Treated         58.2          63.7                5.5 
## 2 Control         60.1          62.4                2.30
# Compute the DiD estimate
treated_change <- 63.7 - 58.2   # = 5.5
control_change <- 62.4 - 60.1   # = 2.3
did_estimate   <- treated_change - control_change  # = 3.2

cat("\nTreated group change: 63.7 - 58.2 =", treated_change, "pp\n")
## 
## Treated group change: 63.7 - 58.2 = 5.5 pp
cat("Control group change: 62.4 - 60.1 =", control_change, "pp\n")
## Control group change: 62.4 - 60.1 = 2.3 pp
cat("DiD estimate:          ", treated_change, "-", control_change,
    "=", did_estimate, "percentage points\n\n")
## DiD estimate:           5.5 - 2.3 = 3.2 percentage points
cat("Interpretation: The job training programme increased employment by",
    did_estimate, "percentage points\n")
## Interpretation: The job training programme increased employment by 3.2 percentage points
cat("on average, above and beyond the", control_change,
    "pp increase that would have occurred anyway (the common time trend).\n")
## on average, above and beyond the 2.3 pp increase that would have occurred anyway (the common time trend).
# Visualise the 2x2 DiD logic
did_plot_df <- bind_rows(
  did_data,
  # Add the counterfactual: treated group following control group's trend
  tibble(
    group    = "Treated (counterfactual)",
    period   = "2018 (post)",
    emp_rate = 58.2 + control_change  # = 60.5: what treated would have been without programme
  )
)

ggplot(
  did_plot_df |> mutate(
    time    = factor(str_extract(period,"\\d{4}"), levels=c("2012","2018")),
    group_f = factor(group, levels=c("Treated","Treated (counterfactual)","Control"))
  ),
  aes(x=time, y=emp_rate, colour=group_f, group=group_f)
) +
  geom_line(aes(linetype=group_f), linewidth=1.3) +
  geom_point(size=5) +
  # Annotate the treatment effect
  annotate("segment",
           x=2, xend=2, y=60.5, yend=63.7,
           arrow=arrow(length=unit(0.3,"cm"), ends="both"),
           colour="#27AE60", linewidth=1.2) +
  annotate("text", x=2.05, y=62.1, label=paste0("Treatment\neffect = ",
           did_estimate, " pp"), hjust=0, colour="#27AE60", size=3.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values=c("Treated"="#C0392B","Treated (counterfactual)"="#C0392B","Control"="#2C6FAC"),
    name=NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values=c("Treated"="solid","Treated (counterfactual)"="dashed","Control"="solid"),
                        name=NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=\(x) paste0(x,"%"), limits=c(55,66)) +
  labs(
    title    = "2×2 DiD: Job Training Programme and Employment",
    subtitle = paste("Dashed red = counterfactual (what treated group would have done without programme).",
                     "\nTreatment effect = gap between observed and counterfactual in 2018."),
    x=NULL, y="Employment rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

6.2 Regression Implementation of DiD

In practice, DiD is estimated by a regression with an interaction term:

# Simulate a small dataset for the regression version
set.seed(2024)
did_reg_data <- tibble(
  id      = 1:200,
  treated = rep(c(1,0), each=100),
  post    = rep(c(0,1,0,1), each=50),
  # Outcome with true treatment effect of 3.2
  outcome = 58 + 2*treated + 2.3*post + 3.2*treated*post + rnorm(200, 0, 3)
)

# The DiD estimator is the coefficient on treated × post
did_model <- lm(outcome ~ treated + post + treated:post, data=did_reg_data)
summary(did_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = outcome ~ treated + post + treated:post, data = did_reg_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -9.5960 -2.0661  0.1064  2.2424  6.5475 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)   58.0785     0.4346 133.641  < 2e-16 ***
## treated        1.6947     0.6146   2.757  0.00638 ** 
## post           2.9354     0.6146   4.776 3.49e-06 ***
## treated:post   2.5087     0.8692   2.886  0.00434 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 3.073 on 196 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.4291, Adjusted R-squared:  0.4204 
## F-statistic: 49.11 on 3 and 196 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
cat("\nDiD interpretation of coefficients:\n")
## 
## DiD interpretation of coefficients:
cat("  (Intercept)  = control group mean in the pre period\n")
##   (Intercept)  = control group mean in the pre period
cat("  treated      = pre-period difference between treated and control (at baseline)\n")
##   treated      = pre-period difference between treated and control (at baseline)
cat("  post         = time trend for the control group (common time effect)\n")
##   post         = time trend for the control group (common time effect)
cat("  treated:post = THE DiD ESTIMATE = treatment effect\n\n")
##   treated:post = THE DiD ESTIMATE = treatment effect
cat("The treated:post coefficient should be ~3.2 (the true effect we simulated).\n")
## The treated:post coefficient should be ~3.2 (the true effect we simulated).
cat("Observed:", round(coef(did_model)["treated:post"], 2), "\n")
## Observed: 2.51

6.3 Event Study / Staggered Treatment

When treatment is adopted at different times by different units, we use an event study design with two-way fixed effects:

set.seed(2024)
n_counties <- 60; n_years <- 10

panel_df <- expand_grid(county=1:n_counties, year=2010:2019) |>
  mutate(
    treated    = county <= 30,
    treat_year = ifelse(!treated, 0L, ifelse(county <= 15, 2013L, 2016L)),
    post       = as.integer(year >= treat_year & treat_year > 0),
    treat_eff  = post * (3.0 + rnorm(n(), 0, 0.3)),
    county_fe  = rep(rnorm(n_counties, 0, 2), each=n_years),
    outcome    = 50 + 0.5*(year-2010) + county_fe + treat_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 1.5)
  )

library(fixest)
es_model <- feols(
  outcome ~ i(year, treated, ref=2012) | county + year,
  data=panel_df, cluster=~county
)

iplot(es_model,
      main  = "Event Study: Treatment Effect by Year (Reference: 2012)",
      xlab  = "Year (2012 = reference)",
      ylab  = "Estimated treatment effect (pp)",
      ci_level = 0.95)
abline(h=0, lty=2, col="grey50")
abline(h=3.0, lty=3, col="#27AE60", lwd=2)
legend("topleft", c("Point estimates + 95% CI","True effect (3.0 pp)"),
       lty=c(1,3), col=c("black","#27AE60"), lwd=2, bty="n")

Reading the event study plot:

  • Points at years before 2012 (the reference year) should be near zero — this is the pre-trend check. If pre-treatment points are far from zero, the parallel trends assumption is violated.
  • Points at years after treatment begins should jump to approximately the true treatment effect.
  • The widening confidence intervals over time reflect smaller samples in early and late years.

7 Part F: Propensity Score Matching

7.1 The Logic

When you cannot randomise and DiD is unavailable (no natural experiment), propensity score matching (PSM) attempts to control confounding by constructing a comparison group that looks similar to the treated group on observed characteristics.

The propensity score \(p(x) = P(\text{treated}=1 | X=x)\) is estimated using logistic regression. Treated and control units with similar propensity scores are matched. After matching, the matched sample should look like a (imperfect) experiment.

Key assumption: No unmeasured confounding. All variables that affect both treatment assignment and the outcome must be in the model. This cannot be tested — it must be justified theoretically.

library(MatchIt)
set.seed(2024)
n <- 1000

obs_data <- tibble(
  ses     = rnorm(n),
  ability = rnorm(n),
  college = rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.3 + 1.0*ses + 0.8*ability)),
  income  = 40 + 8*college + 5*ses + 3*ability + rnorm(n, 0, 5)
)

cat("=== Observational Data ===\n")
## === Observational Data ===
cat("N:", nrow(obs_data), "| True college premium: $8,000\n")
## N: 1000 | True college premium: $8,000
cat("Naive comparison:\n")
## Naive comparison:
obs_data |>
  group_by(college) |>
  summarise(mean_inc=round(mean(income),2), n=n()) |>
  mutate(diff=c(NA, diff(mean_inc))) |>
  print()
## # A tibble: 2 × 4
##   college mean_inc     n  diff
##     <int>    <dbl> <int> <dbl>
## 1       0     37.6   576  NA  
## 2       1     51.2   424  13.6
cat("→ Naive estimate inflated by confounding (high-SES, high-ability more likely to go to college)\n\n")
## → Naive estimate inflated by confounding (high-SES, high-ability more likely to go to college)
# Match on ses and ability (the confounders)
match_out <- matchit(college ~ ses + ability, data=obs_data,
                     method="nearest", ratio=1, replace=FALSE)
summary(match_out)
## 
## Call:
## matchit(formula = college ~ ses + ability, data = obs_data, method = "nearest", 
##     replace = FALSE, ratio = 1)
## 
## Summary of Balance for All Data:
##          Means Treated Means Control Std. Mean Diff. Var. Ratio eCDF Mean
## distance        0.5628        0.3218          1.0868     1.1932    0.2863
## ses             0.4599       -0.3306          0.8653     1.0453    0.2332
## ability         0.3281       -0.1968          0.5461     1.0907    0.1514
##          eCDF Max
## distance   0.4379
## ses        0.3605
## ability    0.2375
## 
## Summary of Balance for Matched Data:
##          Means Treated Means Control Std. Mean Diff. Var. Ratio eCDF Mean
## distance        0.5628        0.3974          0.7459     1.4645    0.1842
## ses             0.4599       -0.0271          0.5331     1.4038    0.1449
## ability         0.3281       -0.0147          0.3566     1.2106    0.1005
##          eCDF Max Std. Pair Dist.
## distance   0.3443          0.7461
## ses        0.2618          0.8308
## ability    0.1769          1.0125
## 
## Sample Sizes:
##           Control Treated
## All           576     424
## Matched       424     424
## Unmatched     152       0
## Discarded       0       0
# Visualise balance: before vs. after matching
match_df <- match.data(match_out)

# Standardised mean differences (SMD) — should be < 0.10 after matching
smd_before <- obs_data |>
  group_by(college) |>
  summarise(m_ses=mean(ses), m_ab=mean(ability)) |>
  summarise(smd_ses=diff(m_ses)/sd(obs_data$ses),
            smd_ab=diff(m_ab)/sd(obs_data$ability))

smd_after <- match_df |>
  group_by(college) |>
  summarise(m_ses=mean(ses), m_ab=mean(ability)) |>
  summarise(smd_ses=diff(m_ses)/sd(match_df$ses),
            smd_ab=diff(m_ab)/sd(match_df$ability))

smd_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(Stage="Before matching", Variable=c("SES","Ability"),
         SMD=c(smd_before$smd_ses, smd_before$smd_ab)),
  tibble(Stage="After matching",  Variable=c("SES","Ability"),
         SMD=c(smd_after$smd_ses, smd_after$smd_ab))
)

ggplot(smd_df, aes(x=abs(SMD), y=Variable, colour=Stage, shape=Stage)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0.10, linetype="dashed", colour="grey50") +
  geom_point(size=5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("Before matching"="#C0392B","After matching"="#27AE60"),
                      name=NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(16,17), name=NULL) +
  annotate("text", x=0.12, y=0.6, label="Threshold: SMD = 0.10",
           hjust=0, colour="grey50", size=3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Covariate Balance: Standardised Mean Differences Before and After Matching",
    subtitle = paste("SMD < 0.10 after matching indicates good balance.",
                     "\nBalance = the matched groups look similar on observed confounders."),
    x="Absolute Standardised Mean Difference (|SMD|)", y=NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom")

# Treatment effect estimate from matched sample
psm_model <- lm(income ~ college, data=match_df, weights=weights)
cat("PSM estimate of college income premium: $",
    round(coef(psm_model)["college"],2), "\n")
## PSM estimate of college income premium: $ 11.56
cat("True effect: $8,000\n")
## True effect: $8,000
cat("Remaining gap reflects imperfect matching or sampling variation.\n")
## Remaining gap reflects imperfect matching or sampling variation.

8 Common Mistakes

Mistake Example Correct Approach
SEM path → causal without assumption check “SEM proves X causes Y” “Under the assumption that [direction], the effect of X on Y is…”
Ignoring equivalent models Only reporting one model specification Check whether alternative causal orderings fit equally well
Using OLS standard errors for indirect effects Reporting “a×b = 0.32, p < .05” using delta method Use bootstrapped CIs for all indirect effects
Controlling for a mediator when estimating total effect Controlling for political knowledge when estimating education → participation Omit mediators when estimating total effects
Controlling for a collider Including “successful applicants” in a study of ability Draw a DAG before deciding what to control for
Parallel trends without verification Reporting DiD without pre-trend plot Always plot pre-treatment trends; include event study
PSM as if it were random assignment “After matching, the groups are equivalent” “After matching on observed covariates, the groups are balanced on those covariates — unmeasured confounding remains possible”

9 Check Your Understanding

1. Run a simple OLS regression of trust on educ using the GSS 2018 data from Part A. Interpret (a) the intercept, (b) the slope, (c) the R², and (d) the F-statistic. Now add age, sex, and income16 as controls. Does the education coefficient change? What does that tell you?

2. In the Political Democracy SEM, dem65 ~ ind60 has a near-zero, non-significant coefficient. A student concludes: “Industrialisation has no effect on 1965 democracy.” Explain precisely why this conclusion is wrong.

3. Draw a DAG representing the relationship between income inequality, social trust, and civic participation. Include at least two confounders and one mediator. Using your DAG, state what variables you would and would not control for to estimate the causal effect of inequality on civic participation.

4. A researcher uses DiD to evaluate a minimum wage increase in some states in 2016. They plot pre-treatment trends and observe that the treated states had slightly higher employment growth from 2013–2015 than the control states. What does this mean for their analysis?

5. 🟥 In a mediation model with X → M → Y, the direct effect (c’) is non-significant but the indirect effect (a×b) is significant. A reviewer argues: “The total effect is significant, so there must be both direct and indirect effects.” Why is this wrong? Construct a numerical example demonstrating full mediation.

sessionInfo()
## R version 4.5.3 (2026-03-11 ucrt)
## Platform: x86_64-w64-mingw32/x64
## Running under: Windows 11 x64 (build 26200)
## 
## Matrix products: default
##   LAPACK version 3.12.1
## 
## locale:
## [1] LC_COLLATE=English_Israel.utf8  LC_CTYPE=English_Israel.utf8   
## [3] LC_MONETARY=English_Israel.utf8 LC_NUMERIC=C                   
## [5] LC_TIME=English_Israel.utf8    
## 
## time zone: Asia/Jerusalem
## tzcode source: internal
## 
## attached base packages:
## [1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     
## 
## other attached packages:
